input.query_abstract: We review theoretical approaches to the understanding of food webs. After an overview of the available food web data, we discuss three different classes of models. The first class comprise static models, which assign links between species according to some simple rule. The second class are dynamical models, which include the population dynamics of several interacting species. We focus on the question of the stability of such webs. The third class are species assembly models and evolutionary models, which build webs starting from a few species by adding new species through a process of "invasion" (assembly models) or "speciation" (evolutionary models). Evolutionary models are found to be capable of building large stable webs.
input.hyp_abstract: Modeling approaches for electric power transmission networks are surveyed, treating buses as nodes and directed real-power flows along transmission corridors as links. After reviewing measured grid topologies and flow records, we organize methods into three categories. The first category is static topology generators, which lay down transmission links between substations by simple stochastic construction rules reproducing empirical line-length, degree, and loading distributions. The second category is dynamical, describing the coupled electromechanical swing behavior of interconnected generators through nonlinear phase-angle equations; we concentrate on stability, establishing when a synchronized operating state is small-signal stable by linearizing about the equilibrium and evaluating the eigenvalues of the reduced system, and we show how added interconnection can either damp or, counterintuitively, undermine synchrony. The third category comprises expansion-planning and self-organized-growth models, in which the network is enlarged incrementally by commissioning new lines and generation, with a reliability-and-cost filter rejecting additions that fail security screening. We find that incremental build-out under such selection can produce large grids that remain transiently and voltage-stable at scale, exhibiting the sparse, hierarchically clustered structure of real interconnections, whereas randomly augmented grids of the same size are markedly more prone to cascading outages. We relate blackout-size statistics to the construction rules that generated the underlying topology.
input.candidate_title: Ising Model on Networks with an Arbitrary Distribution of Connections
input.candidate_abstract: We find the exact critical temperature $T_c$ of the nearest-neighbor ferromagnetic Ising model on an `equilibrium' random graph with an arbitrary degree distribution $P(k)$. We observe an anomalous behavior of the magnetization, magnetic susceptibility and specific heat, when $P(k)$ is fat-tailed, or, loosely speaking, when the fourth moment of the distribution diverges in infinite networks. When the second moment becomes divergent, $T_c$ approaches infinity, the phase transition is of infinite order, and size effect is anomalously strong.
